1	package cmd
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/json"
5		"fmt"
6	
7		"github.com/PanthroCorp-Limited/openclaw-skills/google-workspace/internal/config"
8		"github.com/spf13/cobra"
9	)
10	
11	var configCmd = &cobra.Command{
12		Use:   "config",
13		Short: "Manage service scope configuration",
14	}
15	
16	var configShowCmd = &cobra.Command{
17		Use:   "show",
18		Short: "Display current configuration",
19		Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
20			cfg, err := config.Load(configDir)
21			if err != nil {
22				exitf("loading config: %v", err)
23			}
24	
25			data, _ := json.MarshalIndent(cfg, "", "  ")
26			fmt.Println(string(data))
27			fmt.Printf("\nOAuth scopes: %v\n", cfg.OAuthScopes())
28		},
29	}
30	
31	var (
32		setGmail    *bool
33		setCalendar string
34		setContacts *bool
35	)
36	
37	var configSetCmd = &cobra.Command{
38		Use:   "set",
39		Short: "Update service scope configuration",
40		Long:  "Update which Google services are enabled and their access level.\nAfter changing scopes, re-run 'google-workspace auth login' for the change to take effect.",
41		Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
42			cfg, err := config.Load(configDir)
43			if err != nil {
44				exitf("loading config: %v", err)
45			}
46	
47			if cmd.Flags().Changed("gmail") {
48				cfg.Gmail = *setGmail
49			}
50			if cmd.Flags().Changed("calendar") {
51				cfg.Calendar = config.CalendarMode(setCalendar)
52			}
53			if cmd.Flags().Changed("contacts") {
54				cfg.Contacts = *setContacts
55			}
56	
57			if err := config.Save(configDir, cfg); err != nil {
58				exitf("saving config: %v", err)
59			}
60	
61			data, _ := json.MarshalIndent(cfg, "", "  ")
62			fmt.Println(string(data))
63			fmt.Println("\nConfig saved. Run 'google-workspace auth login' if scopes have changed.")
64		},
65	}
66	
67	func init() {
68		setGmail = configSetCmd.Flags().Bool("gmail", true, "enable Gmail read-only access")
69		configSetCmd.Flags().StringVar(&setCalendar, "calendar", "readonly", "calendar mode: off, readonly, or readwrite")
70		setContacts = configSetCmd.Flags().Bool("contacts", true, "enable Contacts read-only access")
71	
72		configCmd.AddCommand(configShowCmd, configSetCmd)
73		rootCmd.AddCommand(configCmd)
74	}

1	package google
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"fmt"
6	
7		"golang.org/x/oauth2"
8		"google.golang.org/api/option"
9		"google.golang.org/api/people/v1"
10	)
11	
12	// ContactsClient provides read-only access to the Google People API.
13	// No create, update, or delete operations exist in this type.
14	type ContactsClient struct {
15		svc *people.Service
16	}
17	
18	// NewContactsClient creates a People API client using the provided token source.
19	func NewContactsClient(ctx context.Context, ts oauth2.TokenSource) (*ContactsClient, error) {
20		svc, err := people.NewService(ctx, option.WithTokenSource(ts))
21		if err != nil {
22			return nil, fmt.Errorf("creating people service: %w", err)
23		}
24		return &ContactsClient{svc: svc}, nil
25	}
26	
27	// ListContacts returns contacts for the authenticated user.
28	func (c *ContactsClient) ListContacts(ctx context.Context, maxResults int64) ([]*people.Person, error) {
29		call := c.svc.People.Connections.List("people/me").
30			PersonFields("names,emailAddresses,phoneNumbers,organizations").
31			PageSize(maxResults).
32			Context(ctx)
33	
34		resp, err := call.Do()
35		if err != nil {
36			return nil, fmt.Errorf("listing contacts: %w", err)
37		}
38		return resp.Connections, nil
39	}
40	
41	// SearchContacts searches for contacts matching the query.
42	func (c *ContactsClient) SearchContacts(ctx context.Context, query string, maxResults int64) ([]*people.Person, error) {
43		call := c.svc.People.SearchContacts().
44			Query(query).
45			ReadMask("names,emailAddresses,phoneNumbers,organizations").
46			PageSize(int64(maxResults)).
47			Context(ctx)
48	
49		resp, err := call.Do()
50		if err != nil {
51			return nil, fmt.Errorf("searching contacts: %w", err)
52		}
53	
54		var persons []*people.Person
55		for _, result := range resp.Results {
56			persons = append(persons, result.Person)
57		}
58		return persons, nil
59	}
60	
61	// GetContact retrieves a single contact by resource name.
62	func (c *ContactsClient) GetContact(ctx context.Context, resourceName string) (*people.Person, error) {
63		person, err := c.svc.People.Get(resourceName).
64			PersonFields("names,emailAddresses,phoneNumbers,organizations,biographies").
65			Context(ctx).Do()
66		if err != nil {
67			return nil, fmt.Errorf("getting contact %s: %w", resourceName, err)
68		}
69		return person, nil
70	}

21	# Google Workspace Skill
22	
23	Access Gmail (read-only), Google Calendar (configurable), and Google Contacts (read-only).
24	
25	## Installation
26	
27	Download the latest release binary for linux/arm64 and install to `~/.openclaw/bin/`:
28	
29	```bash
30	TAG=$(curl -fsSL "https://api.github.com/repos/PanthroCorp-Limited/openclaw-skills/releases" \
31	  | grep -o '"tag_name":"google-workspace/v[^"]*"' | head -1 | cut -d'"' -f4)
32	VERSION=${TAG#google-workspace/v}
33	curl -fsSL "https://github.com/PanthroCorp-Limited/openclaw-skills/releases/download/${TAG}/google-workspace_${VERSION}_linux_arm64.tar.gz" \
34	  | tar -xz -C ~/.openclaw/bin/ google-workspace
35	chmod +x ~/.openclaw/bin/google-workspace
36	```
37	
38	## Important
39	
40	- Gmail is strictly read-only. You cannot send, modify, or delete emails.
41	- Contacts is strictly read-only. You cannot create, modify, or delete contacts.
42	- Calendar access depends on the configured mode. Check with `google-workspace config show`.
43	
44	## Check configuration
45	
46	Before using any commands, verify what is enabled:
47	
48	```
49	google-workspace config show
50	```
51	
52	## Gmail commands
53	
54	Search messages:
55	```
56	google-workspace gmail search --query "from:someone@example.com" --max-results 10
57	```
58	
59	Read a message by ID:
60	```
61	google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
62	```
63	
64	List labels:
65	```
66	google-workspace gmail labels
67	```
68	
69	Search or read threads:
70	```
71	google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
72	google-workspace gmail threads --id THREAD_ID
73	```

81

85	```
86	
87	Get a specific event:
88	```
89	google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID
90	```
91	
92	Create an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
93	```
94	google-workspace calendar create --summary "Team sync" --start "2026-04-01T10:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T11:00:00Z"
95	```
96	
97	Update an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
98	```
99	google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
100	```
101	
102	Delete an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
103	```
104	google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
105	```
106	
107	## Contacts commands
108	
109	List contacts:
110	```
111	google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50

127	```

131	If the token has expired, ask the operator to re-authenticate by running `google-workspace auth login` on the host.
132

file.content:
---
name: Google Workspace
description: Read-only Gmail and Contacts access with configurable Calendar (readonly or readwrite) for OpenClaw agents
version: 0.2.0
author: panthrocorp
license: MIT-0
metadata:
  openclaw:
    requires:
      env:
        - GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY
        - GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID
        - GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET
      bins:
        - google-workspace
    emoji: "📧"
    homepage: https://github.com/PanthroCorp-Limited/openclaw-skills
    os: ["linux"]
---

# Google Workspace Skill

Access Gmail (read-only), Google Calendar (configurable), and Google Contacts (read-only).

## Installation

Download the latest release binary for linux/arm64 and install to `~/.openclaw/bin/`:

```bash
TAG=$(curl -fsSL "https://api.github.com/repos/PanthroCorp-Limited/openclaw-skills/releases" \
  | grep -o '"tag_name":"google-workspace/v[^"]*"' | head -1 | cut -d'"' -f4)
VERSION=${TAG#google-workspace/v}
curl -fsSL "https://github.com/PanthroCorp-Limited/openclaw-skills/releases/download/${TAG}/google-workspace_${VERSION}_linux_arm64.tar.gz" \
  | tar -xz -C ~/.openclaw/bin/ google-workspace
chmod +x ~/.openclaw/bin/google-workspace
```

## Important

- Gmail is strictly read-only. You cannot send, modify, or delete emails.
- Contacts is strictly read-only. You cannot create, modify, or delete contacts.
- Calendar access depends on the configured mode. Check with `google-workspace config show`.

## Check configuration

Before using any commands, verify what is enabled:

```
google-workspace config show
```

## Gmail commands

Search messages:
```
google-workspace gmail search --query "from:someone@example.com" --max-results 10
```

Read a message by ID:
```
google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
```

List labels:
```
google-workspace gmail labels
```

Search or read threads:
```
google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
google-workspace gmail threads --id THREAD_ID
```

## Calendar commands

List available calendars:
```
google-workspace calendar list
```

List upcoming events:
```
google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-03-29T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-05T23:59:59Z
```

Get a specific event:
```
google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID
```

Create an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
```
google-workspace calendar create --summary "Team sync" --start "2026-04-01T10:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T11:00:00Z"
```

Update an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
```
google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
```

Delete an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
```
google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
```

## Contacts commands

List contacts:
```
google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
```

Search contacts:
```
google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
```

Get a specific contact:
```
google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
```

## Authentication status

Check if the token is valid:
```
google-workspace auth status
```

If the token has expired, ask the operator to re-authenticate by running `google-workspace auth login` on the host.

If authentication fails with `Error 400: policy_enforced`, the operator's Google account likely has Advanced Protection enabled. They will need to temporarily unenroll, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists across sessions.

## Output format

All commands output JSON by default. Use `--output text` for plain text where supported.

For the read-only operations, I should support listing and searching files, retrieving metadata, and downloading file content so the agent can actually read what's in Drive. The scope will be `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.readonly`. Let me outline this plan before diving into the implementation.

content.text:
Here's my plan for adding read-only Google Drive support. Before I start writing code, a few design questions:

**Proposed commands:**
- `drive list` - list files (with optional `--query` for Drive search syntax, `--folder` for folder ID filtering)
- `drive get` - get file metadata by ID
- `drive download` - download/export file content to stdout (essential for an agent to actually read file contents)

**Config:** `Drive bool` (same pattern as Gmail/Contacts, simple on/off, default `true`)

**OAuth scope:** `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.readonly`

**Key question:** For `drive download`, Google Drive has two paths:
1. Binary files (PDFs, images, etc.) use `Files.Get` with `alt=media`
2. Google Docs/Sheets/Slides need `Files.Export` to a specific MIME type (e.g. export a Google Doc as plain text)

I'd suggest the `download` command auto-detects: if the file is a Google Workspace type, export as text/plain (or CSV for Sheets); otherwise stream the raw bytes. This keeps it agent-friendly. Sound right, or do you want to scope it differently?

Also, should the default config have Drive enabled (`true`) like Gmail/Contacts, or disabled (`false`) since adding it changes the OAuth scopes and existing users would need to re-auth?

1	# google-workspace
2	
3	![Language](https://img.shields.io/badge/language-Go-00ADD8?logo=go&logoColor=white)
4	![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT--0-green)
5	![Platform](https://img.shields.io/badge/platform-linux%2Farm64-lightgrey)
6	
7	A custom OpenClaw skill providing read-only Gmail, configurable Calendar, and read-only Contacts access via the Google APIs. Built for environments where the agent instance is treated as potentially hostile.
8	
9	## Why not use an existing skill?
10	
11	Every existing Google-related skill on clawskills.sh requests broad read-write OAuth scopes and most are flagged suspicious by VirusTotal or OpenClaw moderation. This skill enforces strict scope boundaries at three levels: code (no write functions for Gmail/Contacts), config (Calendar write is opt-in), and Google Cloud project (only required APIs enabled).
12	
13	## Services
14	
15	| Service | OAuth Scope | Mode | Write code paths |
16	|---------|------------|------|-----------------|
17	| Gmail | `gmail.readonly` | Read-only | None |
18	| Calendar | `calendar.readonly` or `calendar.events` | Configurable: `off`, `readonly`, `readwrite` | Gated by config check |
19	| Contacts | `contacts.readonly` | Read-only | None |
20	
21	## Installation
22	
23	```bash
24	clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
25	```
26	
27	Or build from source:
28	
29	```bash
30	GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o google-workspace .
31	```
32	
33	## Deployment guide
34	
35	Follow these steps in order to deploy the skill to an OpenClaw instance.
36	
37	### 1. Create a Google Cloud project
38	
39	1. Create a new project in the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/)
40	2. Enable the following APIs only:
41	   - Gmail API
42	   - Google Calendar API
43	   - People API (Contacts)
44	3. Configure the OAuth consent screen (External, but only used by the operator's own account)
45	4. Create an OAuth 2.0 client ID with application type **Desktop**
46	5. Note the **Client ID** and **Client Secret**
47	
48	### 2. Create secrets in Bitwarden Secrets Manager
49	
50	Create three secrets in Bitwarden SM (EU region, PanthroCorp project):
51	
52	| Secret key | Value |
53	|------------|-------|
54	| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Random 64-character hex string (`openssl rand -hex 32`) |
55	| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client ID from step 1 |
56	| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client secret from step 1 |
57	
58	### 3. Add Terraform configuration
59	
60	In the `openclaw` repo, add the Bitwarden secret key references to the instance's tfvars file:
61	
62	```hcl
63	bitwarden_secret_key_google_workspace_token_key = "GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
64	bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_id           = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
65	bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_secret       = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
66	```
67	
68	### 4. Deploy
69	
70	Merge the Terraform changes to `main`. The deploy workflow will:
71	- Create SSM parameters for the three secrets
72	- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
73	- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory
74	
75	### 5. Install the skill on the instance
76	
77	SSH in via Twingate and install:
78	
79	```bash
80	ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
81	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
82	```
83	
84	### 6. Configure scopes
85	
86	```bash
87	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
88	  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
89	```
90	
91	### 7. Authenticate with Google
92	
93	```bash
94	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
95	  google-workspace auth login
96	```
97	
98	1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
99	2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
100	3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
101	4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)
102	
103	**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.
104	
105	### 8. Verify
106	
107	```bash
108	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
109	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
110	```
111	
112	No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.
113	
114	## Prerequisites
115	
116	- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled (see deployment guide above)
117	- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
118	- Three environment variables on the host:
119	  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
120	  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)
121	
122	## Usage
123	
124	### Configure scopes
125	
126	```bash
127	google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
128	google-workspace config show
129	```
130	
131	### Authenticate
132	
133	```bash
134	google-workspace auth login
135	```
136	
137	This prints a URL. Open it in your browser, authorise the requested scopes, then paste the authorisation code back into the terminal.
138	
139	### Gmail (read-only)
140	
141	```bash
142	google-workspace gmail search --query "from:boss@example.com" --max-results 5
143	google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
144	google-workspace gmail labels
145	google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
146	```
147	
148	### Calendar
149	
150	```bash
151	google-workspace calendar list
152	google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-07T23:59:59Z
153	google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID
154	
155	# Only available in readwrite mode:
156	google-workspace calendar create --summary "Standup" --start "2026-04-01T09:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T09:15:00Z"
157	google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
158	google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
159	```
160	
161	### Contacts (read-only)
162	
163	```bash
164	google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
165	google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
166	google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
167	```
168	
169	### Check auth status
170	
171	```bash
172	google-workspace auth status
173	```
174	
175	## Token storage
176	
177	OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key derived via HKDF-SHA256. The encryption key comes from the `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` environment variable, which should be injected from an external secret manager (e.g. Bitwarden SM, AWS SSM Parameter Store). The binary refuses to store tokens if this variable is not set.
178	
179	Default token location: `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/token.enc`
180	
181	## Security
182	
183	- Gmail has no send, modify, or delete code paths. The `internal/google/gmail.go` file only contains `messages.list`, `messages.get`, `labels.list`, and `threads` operations.
184	- Contacts has no create, update, or delete code paths.
185	- Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error if the mode is `readonly`.
186	- The Google Cloud project should only have Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled, providing server-side scope enforcement.
187	- Token encryption uses a random salt per encryption, preventing identical tokens from producing identical ciphertext.
188	
189	## Development
190	
191	```bash
192	go build -o google-workspace .
193	go test ./...
194	go vet ./...
195	```
196	
197	## License
198	
199	[MIT No Attribution](./LICENSE)
200

# google-workspace

![Language](https://img.shields.io/badge/language-Go-00ADD8?logo=go&logoColor=white)
![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT--0-green)
![Platform](https://img.shields.io/badge/platform-linux%2Farm64-lightgrey)

A custom OpenClaw skill providing read-only Gmail, configurable Calendar, and read-only Contacts access via the Google APIs. Built for environments where the agent instance is treated as potentially hostile.

## Why not use an existing skill?

Every existing Google-related skill on clawskills.sh requests broad read-write OAuth scopes and most are flagged suspicious by VirusTotal or OpenClaw moderation. This skill enforces strict scope boundaries at three levels: code (no write functions for Gmail/Contacts), config (Calendar write is opt-in), and Google Cloud project (only required APIs enabled).

## Services

| Service | OAuth Scope | Mode | Write code paths |
|---------|------------|------|-----------------|
| Gmail | `gmail.readonly` | Read-only | None |
| Calendar | `calendar.readonly` or `calendar.events` | Configurable: `off`, `readonly`, `readwrite` | Gated by config check |
| Contacts | `contacts.readonly` | Read-only | None |

## Installation

```bash
clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

Or build from source:

```bash
GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o google-workspace .
```

## Deployment guide

Follow these steps in order to deploy the skill to an OpenClaw instance.

### 1. Create a Google Cloud project

1. Create a new project in the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/)
2. Enable the following APIs only:
   - Gmail API
   - Google Calendar API
   - People API (Contacts)
3. Configure the OAuth consent screen (External, but only used by the operator's own account)
4. Create an OAuth 2.0 client ID with application type **Desktop**
5. Note the **Client ID** and **Client Secret**

### 2. Create secrets in Bitwarden Secrets Manager

Create three secrets in Bitwarden SM (EU region, PanthroCorp project):

| Secret key | Value |
|------------|-------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Random 64-character hex string (`openssl rand -hex 32`) |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client ID from step 1 |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client secret from step 1 |

### 3. Add Terraform configuration

In the `openclaw` repo, add the Bitwarden secret key references to the instance's tfvars file:

```hcl
bitwarden_secret_key_google_workspace_token_key = "GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_id           = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_secret       = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
```

### 4. Deploy

Merge the Terraform changes to `main`. The deploy workflow will:
- Create SSM parameters for the three secrets
- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory

### 5. Install the skill on the instance

SSH in via Twingate and install:

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

### 6. Configure scopes

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
```

### 7. Authenticate with Google

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace auth login
```

1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)

**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.

### 8. Verify

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
```

No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.

## Prerequisites

- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled (see deployment guide above)
- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
- Three environment variables on the host:
  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)

## Usage

### Configure scopes

```bash
google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
google-workspace config show
```

### Authenticate

```bash
google-workspace auth login
```

This prints a URL. Open it in your browser, authorise the requested scopes, then paste the authorisation code back into the terminal.

### Gmail (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace gmail search --query "from:boss@example.com" --max-results 5
google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
google-workspace gmail labels
google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
```

### Calendar

```bash
google-workspace calendar list
google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-07T23:59:59Z
google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID

# Only available in readwrite mode:
google-workspace calendar create --summary "Standup" --start "2026-04-01T09:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T09:15:00Z"
google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
```

### Contacts (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
```

### Check auth status

```bash
google-workspace auth status
```

## Token storage

OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key derived via HKDF-SHA256. The encryption key comes from the `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` environment variable, which should be injected from an external secret manager (e.g. Bitwarden SM, AWS SSM Parameter Store). The binary refuses to store tokens if this variable is not set.

Default token location: `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/token.enc`

## Security

- Gmail has no send, modify, or delete code paths. The `internal/google/gmail.go` file only contains `messages.list`, `messages.get`, `labels.list`, and `threads` operations.
- Contacts has no create, update, or delete code paths.
- Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error if the mode is `readonly`.
- The Google Cloud project should only have Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled, providing server-side scope enforcement.
- Token encryption uses a random salt per encryption, preventing identical tokens from producing identical ciphertext.

## Development

```bash
go build -o google-workspace .
go test ./...
go vet ./...
```

## License

[MIT No Attribution](./LICENSE)

# Google Workspace Skill

Access Gmail (read-only), Google Calendar (configurable), and Google Contacts (read-only).

## Installation

Download the latest release binary for linux/arm64 and install to `~/.openclaw/bin/`:

```bash
TAG=$(curl -fsSL "https://api.github.com/repos/PanthroCorp-Limited/openclaw-skills/releases" \
  | grep -o '"tag_name":"google-workspace/v[^"]*"' | head -1 | cut -d'"' -f4)
VERSION=${TAG#google-workspace/v}
curl -fsSL "https://github.com/PanthroCorp-Limited/openclaw-skills/releases/download/${TAG}/google-workspace_${VERSION}_linux_arm64.tar.gz" \
  | tar -xz -C ~/.openclaw/bin/ google-workspace
chmod +x ~/.openclaw/bin/google-workspace
```

## Important

- Gmail is strictly read-only. You cannot send, modify, or delete emails.
- Contacts is strictly read-only. You cannot create, modify, or delete contacts.
- Calendar access depends on the configured mode. Check with `google-workspace config show`.

## Check configuration

Before using any commands, verify what is enabled:

```
google-workspace config show
```

## Gmail commands

Search messages:
```
google-workspace gmail search --query "from:someone@example.com" --max-results 10
```

Read a message by ID:
```
google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
```

List labels:
```
google-workspace gmail labels
```

Search or read threads:
```
google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
google-workspace gmail threads --id THREAD_ID
```

## Calendar commands

List available calendars:
```
google-workspace calendar list
```

List upcoming events:
```
google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-03-29T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-05T23:59:59Z
```

Get a specific event:
```
google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID
```

Create an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
```
google-workspace calendar create --summary "Team sync" --start "2026-04-01T10:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T11:00:00Z"
```

Update an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
```
google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
```

Delete an event (only if calendar mode is readwrite):
```
google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
```

## Contacts commands

List contacts:
```
google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
```

Search contacts:
```
google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
```

Get a specific contact:
```
google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
```

## Authentication status

Check if the token is valid:
```
google-workspace auth status
```

If the token has expired, ask the operator to re-authenticate by running `google-workspace auth login` on the host.

If authentication fails with `Error 400: policy_enforced`, the operator's Google account likely has Advanced Protection enabled. They will need to temporarily unenroll, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists across sessions.

## Output format

All commands output JSON by default. Use `--output text` for plain text where supported.

# google-workspace

![Language](https://img.shields.io/badge/language-Go-00ADD8?logo=go&logoColor=white)
![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT--0-green)
![Platform](https://img.shields.io/badge/platform-linux%2Farm64-lightgrey)

A custom OpenClaw skill providing read-only Gmail, configurable Calendar, and read-only Contacts access via the Google APIs. Built for environments where the agent instance is treated as potentially hostile.

## Why not use an existing skill?

Every existing Google-related skill on clawskills.sh requests broad read-write OAuth scopes and most are flagged suspicious by VirusTotal or OpenClaw moderation. This skill enforces strict scope boundaries at three levels: code (no write functions for Gmail/Contacts), config (Calendar write is opt-in), and Google Cloud project (only required APIs enabled).

## Services

| Service | OAuth Scope | Mode | Write code paths |
|---------|------------|------|-----------------|
| Gmail | `gmail.readonly` | Read-only | None |
| Calendar | `calendar.readonly` or `calendar.events` | Configurable: `off`, `readonly`, `readwrite` | Gated by config check |
| Contacts | `contacts.readonly` | Read-only | None |

## Installation

```bash
clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

Or build from source:

```bash
GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o google-workspace .
```

## Deployment guide

Follow these steps in order to deploy the skill to an OpenClaw instance.

### 1. Create a Google Cloud project

1. Create a new project in the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/)
2. Enable the following APIs only:
   - Gmail API
   - Google Calendar API
   - People API (Contacts)
3. Configure the OAuth consent screen (External, but only used by the operator's own account)
4. Create an OAuth 2.0 client ID with application type **Desktop**
5. Note the **Client ID** and **Client Secret**

### 2. Create secrets in Bitwarden Secrets Manager

Create three secrets in Bitwarden SM (EU region, PanthroCorp project):

| Secret key | Value |
|------------|-------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Random 64-character hex string (`openssl rand -hex 32`) |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client ID from step 1 |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client secret from step 1 |

### 3. Add Terraform configuration

In the `openclaw` repo, add the Bitwarden secret key references to the instance's tfvars file:

```hcl
bitwarden_secret_key_google_workspace_token_key = "GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_id           = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_secret       = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
```

### 4. Deploy

Merge the Terraform changes to `main`. The deploy workflow will:
- Create SSM parameters for the three secrets
- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory

### 5. Install the skill on the instance

SSH in via Twingate and install:

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

### 6. Configure scopes

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
```

### 7. Authenticate with Google

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace auth login
```

1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)

**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.

### 8. Verify

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
```

No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.

## Prerequisites

- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled (see deployment guide above)
- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
- Three environment variables on the host:
  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)

## Usage

### Configure scopes

```bash
google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
google-workspace config show
```

### Authenticate

```bash
google-workspace auth login
```

This prints a URL. Open it in your browser, authorise the requested scopes, then paste the authorisation code back into the terminal.

### Gmail (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace gmail search --query "from:boss@example.com" --max-results 5
google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
google-workspace gmail labels
google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
```

### Calendar

```bash
google-workspace calendar list
google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-07T23:59:59Z
google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID

# Only available in readwrite mode:
google-workspace calendar create --summary "Standup" --start "2026-04-01T09:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T09:15:00Z"
google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
```

### Contacts (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
```

### Check auth status

```bash
google-workspace auth status
```

## Token storage

OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key derived via HKDF-SHA256. The encryption key comes from the `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` environment variable, which should be injected from an external secret manager (e.g. Bitwarden SM, AWS SSM Parameter Store). The binary refuses to store tokens if this variable is not set.

Default token location: `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/token.enc`

## Security

- Gmail has no send, modify, or delete code paths. The `internal/google/gmail.go` file only contains `messages.list`, `messages.get`, `labels.list`, and `threads` operations.
- Contacts has no create, update, or delete code paths.
- Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error if the mode is `readonly`.
- The Google Cloud project should only have Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled, providing server-side scope enforcement.
- Token encryption uses a random salt per encryption, preventing identical tokens from producing identical ciphertext.

## Development

```bash
go build -o google-workspace .
go test ./...
go vet ./...
```

## License

[MIT No Attribution](./LICENSE)

# google-workspace

![Language](https://img.shields.io/badge/language-Go-00ADD8?logo=go&logoColor=white)
![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT--0-green)
![Platform](https://img.shields.io/badge/platform-linux%2Farm64-lightgrey)

A custom OpenClaw skill providing read-only Gmail, Contacts, and Drive access, plus configurable Calendar, via the Google APIs. Built for environments where the agent instance is treated as potentially hostile.

## Why not use an existing skill?

Every existing Google-related skill on clawskills.sh requests broad read-write OAuth scopes and most are flagged suspicious by VirusTotal or OpenClaw moderation. This skill enforces strict scope boundaries at three levels: code (no write functions for Gmail/Contacts), config (Calendar write is opt-in), and Google Cloud project (only required APIs enabled).

## Services

| Service | OAuth Scope | Mode | Write code paths |
|---------|------------|------|-----------------|
| Gmail | `gmail.readonly` | Read-only | None |
| Calendar | `calendar.readonly` or `calendar.events` | Configurable: `off`, `readonly`, `readwrite` | Gated by config check |
| Contacts | `contacts.readonly` | Read-only | None |

## Installation

```bash
clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

Or build from source:

```bash
GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o google-workspace .
```

## Deployment guide

Follow these steps in order to deploy the skill to an OpenClaw instance.

### 1. Create a Google Cloud project

1. Create a new project in the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/)
2. Enable the following APIs only:
   - Gmail API
   - Google Calendar API
   - People API (Contacts)
3. Configure the OAuth consent screen (External, but only used by the operator's own account)
4. Create an OAuth 2.0 client ID with application type **Desktop**
5. Note the **Client ID** and **Client Secret**

### 2. Create secrets in Bitwarden Secrets Manager

Create three secrets in Bitwarden SM (EU region, PanthroCorp project):

| Secret key | Value |
|------------|-------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Random 64-character hex string (`openssl rand -hex 32`) |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client ID from step 1 |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client secret from step 1 |

### 3. Add Terraform configuration

In the `openclaw` repo, add the Bitwarden secret key references to the instance's tfvars file:

```hcl
bitwarden_secret_key_google_workspace_token_key = "GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_id           = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_secret       = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
```

### 4. Deploy

Merge the Terraform changes to `main`. The deploy workflow will:
- Create SSM parameters for the three secrets
- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory

### 5. Install the skill on the instance

SSH in via Twingate and install:

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

### 6. Configure scopes

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
```

### 7. Authenticate with Google

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace auth login
```

1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)

**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.

### 8. Verify

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
```

No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.

## Prerequisites

- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled (see deployment guide above)
- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
- Three environment variables on the host:
  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)

4. Create an OAuth 2.0 client ID with application type **Desktop**
5. Note the **Client ID** and **Client Secret**

### 2. Create secrets in Bitwarden Secrets Manager

Create three secrets in Bitwarden SM (EU region, PanthroCorp project):

| Secret key | Value |
|------------|-------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Random 64-character hex string (`openssl rand -hex 32`) |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client ID from step 1 |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client secret from step 1 |

### 3. Add Terraform configuration

In the `openclaw` repo, add the Bitwarden secret key references to the instance's tfvars file:

```hcl
bitwarden_secret_key_google_workspace_token_key = "GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_id           = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_secret       = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
```

### 4. Deploy

Merge the Terraform changes to `main`. The deploy workflow will:
- Create SSM parameters for the three secrets
- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory

### 5. Install the skill on the instance

SSH in via Twingate and install:

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

### 6. Configure scopes

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
```

### 7. Authenticate with Google

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace auth login
```

1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)

**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.

### 8. Verify

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
```

No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.

## Prerequisites

- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled (see deployment guide above)
- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
- Three environment variables on the host:
  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)

## Usage

### Configure scopes

```bash
google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
google-workspace config show
```

### Authenticate

```bash
google-workspace auth login
```

This prints a URL. Open it in your browser, authorise the requested scopes, then paste the authorisation code back into the terminal.

### Gmail (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace gmail search --query "from:boss@example.com" --max-results 5
google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
google-workspace gmail labels
google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
```

### Calendar

```bash
google-workspace calendar list
google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-07T23:59:59Z
google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID

# Only available in readwrite mode:
google-workspace calendar create --summary "Standup" --start "2026-04-01T09:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T09:15:00Z"
google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
```

### Contacts (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
```

### Check auth status

```bash
google-workspace auth status
```

## Token storage

OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key derived via HKDF-SHA256. The encryption key comes from the `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` environment variable, which should be injected from an external secret manager (e.g. Bitwarden SM, AWS SSM Parameter Store). The binary refuses to store tokens if this variable is not set.

Default token location: `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/token.enc`

## Security

- Gmail has no send, modify, or delete code paths. The `internal/google/gmail.go` file only contains `messages.list`, `messages.get`, `labels.list`, and `threads` operations.
- Contacts has no create, update, or delete code paths.
- Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error if the mode is `readonly`.
- The Google Cloud project should only have Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled, providing server-side scope enforcement.
- Token encryption uses a random salt per encryption, preventing identical tokens from producing identical ciphertext.

## Development

```bash
go build -o google-workspace .
go test ./...
go vet ./...
```

## License

[MIT No Attribution](./LICENSE)

```hcl
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_id           = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_secret       = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
```

### 4. Deploy

Merge the Terraform changes to `main`. The deploy workflow will:
- Create SSM parameters for the three secrets
- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory

### 5. Install the skill on the instance

SSH in via Twingate and install:

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

### 6. Configure scopes

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true --drive=true
```

### 7. Authenticate with Google

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace auth login
```

1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)

**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.

### 8. Verify

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
```

No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.

## Prerequisites

- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, People API, and Google Drive API enabled (see deployment guide above)
- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
- Three environment variables on the host:
  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)

## Usage

### Configure scopes

```bash
google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
google-workspace config show
```

### Authenticate

```bash
google-workspace auth login
```

This prints a URL. Open it in your browser, authorise the requested scopes, then paste the authorisation code back into the terminal.

### Gmail (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace gmail search --query "from:boss@example.com" --max-results 5
google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
google-workspace gmail labels
google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
```

### Calendar

```bash
google-workspace calendar list
google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-07T23:59:59Z
google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID

# Only available in readwrite mode:
google-workspace calendar create --summary "Standup" --start "2026-04-01T09:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T09:15:00Z"
google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
```

### Contacts (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
```

### Check auth status

```bash
google-workspace auth status
```

## Token storage

OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key derived via HKDF-SHA256. The encryption key comes from the `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` environment variable, which should be injected from an external secret manager (e.g. Bitwarden SM, AWS SSM Parameter Store). The binary refuses to store tokens if this variable is not set.

Default token location: `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/token.enc`

## Security

- Gmail has no send, modify, or delete code paths. The `internal/google/gmail.go` file only contains `messages.list`, `messages.get`, `labels.list`, and `threads` operations.
- Contacts has no create, update, or delete code paths.
- Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error if the mode is `readonly`.
- The Google Cloud project should only have Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled, providing server-side scope enforcement.
- Token encryption uses a random salt per encryption, preventing identical tokens from producing identical ciphertext.

## Development

```bash
go build -o google-workspace .
go test ./...
go vet ./...
```

## License

[MIT No Attribution](./LICENSE)

## Why not use an existing skill?

Every existing Google-related skill on clawskills.sh requests broad read-write OAuth scopes and most are flagged suspicious by VirusTotal or OpenClaw moderation. |---------|------------|------|-----------------|
| Gmail | `gmail.readonly` | Read-only | None |
| Calendar | `calendar.readonly` or `calendar.events` | Configurable: `off`, `readonly`, `readwrite` | Gated by config check |

### 1. Create a Google Cloud project

content.text: Now add the upgrade/re-auth instructions. I'll add a section for existing users.

input.old_string: ## Prerequisites
input.new_string:
## Upgrading from a previous version

If you are upgrading from a version that did not include Drive support, you need to:

1. Enable the **Google Drive API** in your Google Cloud project at [console.cloud.google.com/apis/library/drive.googleapis.com](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/library/drive.googleapis.com)
2. Re-authenticate to obtain a token with the new `drive.readonly` scope:
   ```bash
   google-workspace auth login
   ```

The new `drive` config field defaults to `true`. If you do not want Drive access, disable it before re-authenticating:

```bash
google-workspace config set --drive=false
```

## Prerequisites

## Upgrading from a previous version

If you are upgrading from a version that did not include Drive support, you need to:

1. Enable the **Google Drive API** in your Google Cloud project at [console.cloud.google.com/apis/library/drive.googleapis.com](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/library/drive.googleapis.com)
2. Re-authenticate to obtain a token with the new `drive.readonly` scope:
   ```bash
   google-workspace auth login
   ```

The new `drive` config field defaults to `true`. If you do not want Drive access, disable it before re-authenticating:

```bash
google-workspace config set --drive=false
```

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# google-workspace

![Language](https://img.shields.io/badge/language-Go-00ADD8?logo=go&logoColor=white)
![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT--0-green)
![Platform](https://img.shields.io/badge/platform-linux%2Farm64-lightgrey)

A custom OpenClaw skill providing read-only Gmail, Contacts, and Drive access, plus configurable Calendar, via the Google APIs. Built for environments where the agent instance is treated as potentially hostile.

## Why not use an existing skill?

Every existing Google-related skill on clawskills.sh requests broad read-write OAuth scopes and most are flagged suspicious by VirusTotal or OpenClaw moderation. This skill enforces strict scope boundaries at three levels: code (no write functions for Gmail/Contacts/Drive), config (Calendar write is opt-in), and Google Cloud project (only required APIs enabled).

## Services

| Service | OAuth Scope | Mode | Write code paths |
|---------|------------|------|-----------------|
| Gmail | `gmail.readonly` | Read-only | None |
| Calendar | `calendar.readonly` or `calendar.events` | Configurable: `off`, `readonly`, `readwrite` | Gated by config check |
| Contacts | `contacts.readonly` | Read-only | None |
| Drive | `drive.readonly` | Read-only | None |

## Installation

```bash
clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

Or build from source:

```bash
GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o google-workspace .
```

## Deployment guide

Follow these steps in order to deploy the skill to an OpenClaw instance.

### 1. Create a Google Cloud project

1. Create a new project in the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/)
2. Enable the following APIs only:
   - Gmail API
   - Google Calendar API
   - People API (Contacts)
   - Google Drive API
3. Configure the OAuth consent screen (External, but only used by the operator's own account)
4. Create an OAuth 2.0 client ID with application type **Desktop**
5. Note the **Client ID** and **Client Secret**

### 2. Create secrets in Bitwarden Secrets Manager

Create three secrets in Bitwarden SM (EU region, PanthroCorp project):

| Secret key | Value |
|------------|-------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Random 64-character hex string (`openssl rand -hex 32`) |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client ID from step 1 |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client secret from step 1 |

### 3. Add Terraform configuration

In the `openclaw` repo, add the Bitwarden secret key references to the instance's tfvars file:

```hcl
bitwarden_secret_key_google_workspace_token_key = "GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_id           = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_secret       = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
```

### 4. Deploy

Merge the Terraform changes to `main`. The deploy workflow will:
- Create SSM parameters for the three secrets
- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory

### 5. Install the skill on the instance

SSH in via Twingate and install:

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

### 6. Configure scopes

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true --drive=true
```

### 7. Authenticate with Google

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace auth login
```

1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)

**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.

### 8. Verify

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
```

No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.

## Prerequisites

- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, People API, and Google Drive API enabled (see deployment guide above)
- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
- Three environment variables on the host:
  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)

## Usage

### Configure scopes

```bash
google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true --drive=true
google-workspace config show
```

### Authenticate

```bash
google-workspace auth login
```

This prints a URL. Open it in your browser, authorise the requested scopes, then paste the authorisation code back into the terminal.

### Gmail (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace gmail search --query "from:boss@example.com" --max-results 5
google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
google-workspace gmail labels
google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
```

### Calendar

```bash
google-workspace calendar list
google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-07T23:59:59Z
google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID

# Only available in readwrite mode:
google-workspace calendar create --summary "Standup" --start "2026-04-01T09:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T09:15:00Z"
google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
```

### Contacts (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
```

### Drive (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace drive list --max-results 20
google-workspace drive list --query "name contains 'report'" --max-results 10
google-workspace drive get --id FILE_ID
google-workspace drive download --id FILE_ID
```

Google Docs are exported as plain text, Sheets as CSV, and Slides as plain text. All other files download as raw bytes.

### Check auth status

```bash
google-workspace auth status
```

## Token storage

OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key derived via HKDF-SHA256. The encryption key comes from the `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` environment variable, which should be injected from an external secret manager (e.g. Bitwarden SM, AWS SSM Parameter Store). The binary refuses to store tokens if this variable is not set.

Default token location: `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/token.enc`

## Security

- Gmail has no send, modify, or delete code paths. The `internal/google/gmail.go` file only contains `messages.list`, `messages.get`, `labels.list`, and `threads` operations.
- Contacts has no create, update, or delete code paths.
- Drive has no create, update, or delete code paths. Only file listing, metadata retrieval, and content download/export are supported.
- Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error if the mode is `readonly`.
- The Google Cloud project should only have Gmail API, Calendar API, People API, and Google Drive API enabled, providing server-side scope enforcement.
- Token encryption uses a random salt per encryption, preventing identical tokens from producing identical ciphertext.

## Development

```bash
go build -o google-workspace .
go test ./...
go vet ./...
```

## License

[MIT No Attribution](./LICENSE)

lines: No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.
+If you are upgrading from a version that did not include Drive support, you need to:
+1. Enable the **Google Drive API** in your Google Cloud project at [console.cloud.google.com/apis/library/drive.googleapis.com](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/library/drive.googleapis.com)
+2. Re-authenticate to obtain a token with the new `drive.readonly` scope:
+The new `drive` config field defaults to `true`. If you do not want Drive access, disable it before re-authenticating:
- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, People API, and Google Drive API enabled (see deployment guide above)

file.content:
# google-workspace

## Overview

Read-only Gmail and Contacts skill with configurable Calendar access. Security-first design: write code paths for Gmail and Contacts do not exist in the codebase.

## Architecture

- `cmd/` — Cobra CLI. Service commands (`gmail`, `calendar`, `contacts`, `auth`, `config`) register on `rootCmd`. Helper functions `gmailClient()`, `calendarClient()`, `contactsClient()` handle config loading, scope validation, token decryption, and API client construction inline.
- `internal/config/config.go` — JSON config model with three fields: `Gmail bool`, `Contacts bool`, and `Calendar CalendarMode` (`off`, `readonly`, `readwrite`). `CalendarMode` is the only multi-state gate; Gmail and Contacts are simple on/off booleans. `Config.OAuthScopes()` derives the OAuth scope list from the current config; scopes expand as services are enabled. Changing any value requires re-authentication to issue a new token with the updated scopes.
- `internal/oauth/` — OAuth2 Desktop flow with a localhost redirect. `InteractiveLogin` prompts for the **code value only** (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=` in the redirect URL). The browser fails to load `http://localhost`, and the operator copies the code parameter value from the URL bar. This differs from zoho-mail, which takes the full redirect URL.
- `internal/google/` — Thin typed wrappers around Google API services. Each wrapper exposes only the operations the skill permits: `GmailClient` has no send/modify/delete; `ContactsClient` has no write operations.
- `internal/crypto/` — AES-256-GCM with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation. Wire format: `salt (16B) || nonce (12B) || ciphertext+tag`.

## Scope enforcement (three layers)

1. Code: write methods do not exist in `internal/google/`.
2. Config: Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error otherwise.
3. Google Cloud project: only Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API should be enabled, providing server-side enforcement.

## Required environment variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` | Passphrase for AES-256-GCM token encryption |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` | Google OAuth2 client ID |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | Google OAuth2 client secret |

Config directory defaults to `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/`. Override with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_CONFIG_DIR` or `--config-dir`.

## OAuth flow note

`InteractiveLogin` expects only the `code` value from the redirect URL, not the full URL. After the browser fails to load `http://localhost`, the operator copies the value of the `code=` parameter only and pastes it into the terminal. See `internal/oauth/oauth.go` for the implementation.

## Calendar mode and scope changes

If `CalendarMode` changes (e.g. `readonly` to `readwrite`), the stored token was issued with the old scopes. The operator must run `google-workspace auth login` again to issue a new token with the expanded scopes. The binary does not detect scope mismatches at runtime.

# google-workspace

## Overview

Read-only Gmail and Contacts skill with configurable Calendar access. Security-first design: write code paths for Gmail and Contacts do not exist in the codebase.

## Architecture

- `cmd/` — Cobra CLI. Service commands (`gmail`, `calendar`, `contacts`, `auth`, `config`) register on `rootCmd`. Helper functions `gmailClient()`, `calendarClient()`, `contactsClient()` handle config loading, scope validation, token decryption, and API client construction inline.
- `internal/config/config.go` — JSON config model with three fields: `Gmail bool`, `Contacts bool`, and `Calendar CalendarMode` (`off`, `readonly`, `readwrite`). `CalendarMode` is the only multi-state gate; Gmail and Contacts are simple on/off booleans. `Config.OAuthScopes()` derives the OAuth scope list from the current config; scopes expand as services are enabled. Changing any value requires re-authentication to issue a new token with the updated scopes.
- `internal/oauth/` — OAuth2 Desktop flow with a localhost redirect. `InteractiveLogin` prompts for the **code value only** (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=` in the redirect URL). The browser fails to load `http://localhost`, and the operator copies the code parameter value from the URL bar. This differs from zoho-mail, which takes the full redirect URL.
- `internal/google/` — Thin typed wrappers around Google API services. Each wrapper exposes only the operations the skill permits: `GmailClient` has no send/modify/delete; `ContactsClient` has no write operations.
- `internal/crypto/` — AES-256-GCM with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation. Wire format: `salt (16B) || nonce (12B) || ciphertext+tag`.

## Scope enforcement (three layers)

1. Code: write methods do not exist in `internal/google/`.
2. Config: Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error otherwise.
3. Google Cloud project: only Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API should be enabled, providing server-side enforcement.

## Required environment variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` | Passphrase for AES-256-GCM token encryption |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` | Google OAuth2 client ID |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | Google OAuth2 client secret |

Config directory defaults to `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/`. Override with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_CONFIG_DIR` or `--config-dir`.

## OAuth flow note

`InteractiveLogin` expects only the `code` value from the redirect URL, not the full URL. After the browser fails to load `http://localhost`, the operator copies the value of the `code=` parameter only and pastes it into the terminal. See `internal/oauth/oauth.go` for the implementation.

## Calendar mode and scope changes

If `CalendarMode` changes (e.g. `readonly` to `readwrite`), the stored token was issued with the old scopes. The operator must run `google-workspace auth login` again to issue a new token with the expanded scopes. The binary does not detect scope mismatches at runtime.

# google-workspace

## Overview

Read-only Gmail, Contacts, and Drive skill with configurable Calendar access. Security-first design: write code paths for Gmail, Contacts, and Drive do not exist in the codebase.

## Architecture

- `cmd/` — Cobra CLI. Service commands (`gmail`, `calendar`, `contacts`, `drive`, `auth`, `config`) register on `rootCmd`. Helper functions `gmailClient()`, `calendarClient()`, `contactsClient()`, `driveClient()` handle config loading, scope validation, token decryption, and API client construction inline.
- `internal/config/config.go` — JSON config model with four fields: `Gmail bool`, `Contacts bool`, `Drive bool`, and `Calendar CalendarMode` (`off`, `readonly`, `readwrite`). `CalendarMode` is the only multi-state gate; Gmail, Contacts, and Drive are simple on/off booleans. `Config.OAuthScopes()` derives the OAuth scope list from the current config; scopes expand as services are enabled. Changing any value requires re-authentication to issue a new token with the updated scopes.
- `internal/oauth/` — OAuth2 Desktop flow with a localhost redirect. `InteractiveLogin` prompts for the **code value only** (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=` in the redirect URL). The browser fails to load `http://localhost`, and the operator copies the code parameter value from the URL bar. This differs from zoho-mail, which takes the full redirect URL.
- `internal/google/` — Thin typed wrappers around Google API services. Each wrapper exposes only the operations the skill permits: `GmailClient` has no send/modify/delete; `ContactsClient` has no write operations.
- `internal/crypto/` — AES-256-GCM with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation. Wire format: `salt (16B) || nonce (12B) || ciphertext+tag`.

## Scope enforcement (three layers)

1. Code: write methods do not exist in `internal/google/`.
2. Config: Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error otherwise.
3. Google Cloud project: only Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API should be enabled, providing server-side enforcement.

## Required environment variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` | Passphrase for AES-256-GCM token encryption |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` | Google OAuth2 client ID |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | Google OAuth2 client secret |

Config directory defaults to `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/`. Override with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_CONFIG_DIR` or `--config-dir`.

## OAuth flow note

`InteractiveLogin` expects only the `code` value from the redirect URL, not the full URL. After the browser fails to load `http://localhost`, the operator copies the value of the `code=` parameter only and pastes it into the terminal. See `internal/oauth/oauth.go` for the implementation.

## Calendar mode and scope changes

If `CalendarMode` changes (e.g. `readonly` to `readwrite`), the stored token was issued with the old scopes. The operator must run `google-workspace auth login` again to issue a new token with the expanded scopes. The binary does not detect scope mismatches at runtime.

All done. Here's a summary of what was added:

**New files:**
- `internal/google/drive.go` - read-only Drive client with `ListFiles`, `GetFile`, and `DownloadFile` (auto-detects Google Workspace files and exports Docs as plain text, Sheets as CSV, Slides as plain text; all other files download as raw bytes)

**Modified files:**
- `internal/config/config.go` - added `Drive bool` field, `drive.readonly` scope, default `true`
- `internal/config/config_test.go` - updated all test cases to include Drive
- `cmd/config.go` - added `--drive` flag to `config set`
- `cmd/root.go` - updated Short description
- `SKILL.md` - updated description, added Drive commands section
- `CLAUDE.md` - updated architecture docs
- `README.md` - added Drive to services table, GCP setup, upgrade instructions, usage section, and security section
- `go.mod` / `go.sum` - `google.golang.org/api` bumped to v0.273.1

**Upgrade path for existing instances** is documented in the new "Upgrading from a previous version" section in the README: enable the Google Drive API in the GCP project, then re-run `auth login` to get a token with the new scope.

# gh-ship: Branch → Commit → PR → CI → Merge → CI

> **GitHub repos only.** Before doing anything, confirm the repo has a GitHub remote (`git remote -v`).
> If the remote is Azure DevOps or anything other than `github.com`, stop and tell the user this command does not apply — use the ADO MCP tools instead.

Ship the current working changes end-to-end on GitHub. Run each phase in order, stopping if anything fails.

## Phase 1: Branch

Check current branch with `git branch --show-current`.

- If on `main` or `master`: create a feature branch. Derive the name from the staged changes or `` if provided. Use kebab-case, prefixed with `fix/` or `feat/` per commit convention. Ask if unclear.
- If already on a feature branch: continue.

## Phase 2: Commit

Run `git status` and `git diff --cached` to understand what is staged.

- If nothing is staged: run `git diff --stat` to show unstaged changes, then ask which files to stage.
- Stage and commit following the project commit convention (semantic: `fix:`, `feat:`, `breaking:`; subject ≤50 chars; bullet body with emojis). Do not skip pre-commit hooks (`--no-verify` is forbidden unless the user explicitly requests it).
- If pre-commit hooks modify files, re-stage and commit again.

## Phase 3: Push & PR

Push the branch: `git push -u origin <branch>`.

Create the PR using `gh pr create`. Follow the project PR format:
- Short title (≤70 chars)
- Body with `## Summary` bullets and no test plan section
- Add `🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/claude-code)` footer

Show the PR URL.

## Phase 4: CI Check (post-PR)

Wait for CI to complete: `gh run list --branch <branch> --limit 5` then watch the most recent run with `gh run watch <run-id>`.

- If CI passes: proceed to Phase 5.
- If CI fails: show the failed job logs (`gh run view <run-id> --log-failed`), diagnose, fix, commit, push. Re-run Phase 4.

## Phase 5: Merge

Once CI is green: `gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch` (use squash unless the user specifies otherwise).

Confirm the merge completed and the branch was deleted.

## Phase 6: CI Check (post-merge)

Run `gh run list --branch main --limit 3` and watch the post-merge run.

- If CI passes: done. Report the merged PR URL and final status.
- If CI fails: report the failure with logs. Do not attempt auto-fix on main — flag it for the user.

## Notes

- `` can provide a branch name, PR title hint, or both.
- Never force-push, never skip hooks, never push directly to main/master.
- If at any point the user needs to take a manual action, stop and clearly state what is needed.

file.content:
name: Dependabot Auto-Merge

on:
  pull_request:

permissions:
  contents: write
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  auto-merge:
    if: github.actor == 'dependabot[bot]'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Approve PR
        run: gh pr review --approve "$PR_URL"
        env:
          PR_URL: ${{ github.event.pull_request.html_url }}
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Enable auto-merge
        run: gh pr merge --auto --squash "$PR_URL"
        env:
          PR_URL: ${{ github.event.pull_request.html_url }}
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
